teaching.summary: Every Tongan sentence follows a fixed order: tense marker + pronoun + verb. The verb never changes form — the tense marker does all the work.

key_rules.rule: Sentences always start with a tense marker
The pronoun goes between the tense marker and the verb
Questions use the same word order — just change your intonation

title: Tense Markers and Pronouns
preview: Four tense markers, full pronoun paradigm

teaching.summary: Tongan has four tense markers that fill the same position. The pronoun for "I" changes depending on which tense marker you use. Tongan also distinguishes dual (two people) from plural (three or more) and inclusive "we" (you too) from exclusive "we" (not you).

key_rules.rule: Four tense markers: naʻa (past), ʻoku (present), kuo (perfect), te (future)
The "I" pronoun changes with the tense marker: ku (naʻa), ou (ʻoku), u (kuo/te)
Tau (inclusive) means "we" including the listener; mau (exclusive) means "we" without the listener
Tau or ta without a tense marker makes a suggestion

title: Descriptive Words
preview: Adjectives as verbs, modifiers, pē and ʻaupito

teaching.summary: In Tongan, adjectives fill the verb slot — no helper verb like "am" or "is" is needed. Modifiers like lelei (well), lahi (a lot), and ʻaupito (very) go after the verb to describe how or how much.

key_rules.rule: Adjectives work like verbs: TM + pronoun + adjective
Modifiers go after the verb or adjective
ʻAupito (very) always comes last
Bare-noun objects attach directly to the verb

title: Time Words
topics: Yesterday, last night, a while ago
preview: Time word sits at the end of the sentence

teaching.summary: Time words go at the very end of the sentence. Past time words start with ʻane- and future time words start with ʻa-.

key_rules.rule: Time words always go at the end of the sentence
Past: ʻaneafi (yesterday), ʻanepō (last night), ʻanenai (a while ago)
key_rules.example_english: We ate taro a while ago.

key_rules.rule: Future: ʻapongipongi (tomorrow), ʻapō (tonight), ʻanai (in a while)

teaching.summary: Commands use the bare verb alone with no tense marker. For groups, add mou before the verb. To say "don’t," use ʻoua te + pronoun + verb. The polite particle muʻa means "please."

key_rules.rule: Commands to one person: just the verb
Commands to a group: mou + verb
Prohibitions: ʻoua te + pronoun + verb
Muʻa adds politeness ("please")

title: Location
preview: ʻi + place · ʻi fē · heni / hena / hē

teaching.summary: The preposition ʻi means "in," "at," or "on." Use it to say where someone is or where something happens. Three demonstratives point to places: heni (here), hena (there near you), hē (over there).

key_rules.rule: Say where someone is: TM + pronoun + ʻi + place
Ask where with ʻi fē
Three demonstratives: heni (here), hena (there), hē (over there)

teaching.summary: Ki means "to" (destination) and mei means "from" (origin). Before a person’s name, prepositions add -a (kia, meia). Before pronouns, they add -ate (kiate, meiate). The verbs mahino (understand) and ngalo (forget) use these prepositional pronouns.

key_rules.rule: Ki marks where you’re going; mei marks where you came from
Before a person: ki → kia, mei → meia, ʻi → ʻia
Before a pronoun: ki → kiate, mei → meiate, ʻi → ʻiate

teaching.summary: Tongan has two articles: ha (a, some) for indefinite nouns and e (the) for definite nouns. After the prepositions ʻi, ki, and mei, the definite article changes to he.

key_rules.rule: Ha means "a" or "some" (indefinite, non-specific)
E means "the" (definite); he after prepositions ʻi, ki, mei
Three ways to use nouns: bare (kai mā), indefinite (kai ha mā), definite (kai e mā)
key_rules.example_tongan: Tē u ʻalu ki he fale.
key_rules.example_english: I will go to the house.

paradigm.caption: Noun Forms with Articles
rows: kai ha mā — eat some bread
kai e mā — eat the bread

teaching.summary: To negate a sentence, insert ʻikai te between the tense marker and the pronoun. Important: the tense marker changes in negation — use naʻe for past (not naʻa) and ʻe for future (not te). For weather and impersonal verbs with no pronoun, use ʻikai ke instead.

key_rules.rule: Negation with a pronoun: TM + ʻikai te + pronoun + verb
Tense markers change: naʻe (not naʻa) for past, ʻe (not te) for future
Impersonal negation (no pronoun): TM + ʻikai ke + verb

title: The Comitative Mo
preview: mo + name / pronoun (with, and, taking along)

teaching.summary: Mo means "with" (together with) and also "and" between names. It goes after the verb and before the companion’s name or pronoun.

key_rules.rule: Mo + name means "with" (accompaniment)
Mo between names means "and"
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻa ku lea kia Sione mo Mele.
key_rules.example_english: I spoke to John and Mary.

key_rules.rule: Mo + verb makes a command to exactly two people

title: Question Words
preview: ʻi fē · fīha · fēfē · hā — sits where the answer goes

teaching.summary: Question words sit where the answer would go in the sentence. Use ʻanefē to ask "when" about the past, ʻafē for the future, and fēfē for "how."

key_rules.rule: Past "when": ʻanefē (goes where the time word answer would be)
Future "when": ʻafē
"How": fēfē asks about condition or manner

teaching.summary: The ko pattern identifies things without using a tense marker or verb. Ko e + noun + demonstrative says "this is a..." Three demonstratives: ʻeni (this, near me), ʻena (that, near you), ē (over there).

key_rules.rule: Identify things: ko e + noun + demonstrative
Ask "what is that?": ko e hā + demonstrative
Negate with ʻoku ʻikai ko e
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻikai ko e hele ʻeni.

paradigm.caption: Ko Patterns
rows: ʻOku ʻikai ko e hele ʻeni.

title: Ko Questions
preview: ko hai · ko e hā · ko fē · ko e hā…ai

teaching.summary: Ko hai asks "who?" and ko fē asks "where?" Both use the ko pattern. Answers use ko + noun.

key_rules.rule: "Who?": ko hai + tense marker + verb
"Where?": ko fē + noun
Answers use ko + name or koʻeni (here it is)

title: Greetings and Social Formulas
preview: Mālō e lelei · ʻalu ā · nofo ā

teaching.summary: Tongan greetings are fixed formulas. Mālō e lelei is the main greeting, fēfē hake asks "how are you?", and farewells depend on who is leaving: ʻalu ā (to someone going) or nofo ā (to someone staying).

key_rules.rule: Greeting: Mālō e lelei! (response: repeat it)
"How are you?": Fēfē hake? Answer: Sai pē!
Farewells: ʻalu ā (to someone leaving), nofo ā (to someone staying)
Ask someone’s name: Ko hai ho hingoa?

teaching.summary: When the subject is a name (not a pronoun), it moves after the verb and is marked with ʻa. The tense marker also changes: naʻa becomes naʻe, and te becomes ʻe. Weather expressions like ʻuha (rain) have no subject at all.

key_rules.rule: Noun subject word order: TM + verb + ʻa + name
Tense markers change: naʻa → naʻe, te → ʻe (before noun subjects)
Weather verbs have no subject: just TM + verb

paradigm.caption: Pronoun vs. Noun Subject
rows: ʻOku ʻalu ʻa Sione. (John is going.)
ʻE hiva ʻa Sione. (John will sing.)

title: Equational Sentences
topics: I am a teacher (ko e + noun + pronoun)
preview: ko e + predicate + ʻa + noun subject

teaching.summary: Equational sentences use ko e to identify what someone or something is, without a tense marker or verb. The subject appears at the end, marked by ʻa for proper nouns or as a bare pronoun.

key_rules.rule: With a noun subject, use ʻa before the name: ko e + noun + ʻa + name
With a pronoun subject, drop ʻa: ko e + noun + pronoun
Subject-first order emphasizes who: ko + subject + ko e + predicate
key_rules.example_english: Lisiate is the teacher. (emphasis on Lisiate)

paradigm.caption: Equational Sentence Patterns
rows: Ko e taʻahine mālohi ʻa Seini.
Ko Peau ko e tangata mālohi.
ʻOku ʻikai ko e faiako au.

title: Possessives
preview: ʻeku / hoku classes · ʻa (doer) and ʻo (receiver)

teaching.summary: Tongan splits possession into two classes. ʻE-class covers things you control, create, or consume. Ho-class covers things that shelter, clothe, or define you. Each class has its own set of pronouns.

key_rules.rule: ʻE-class for things you act upon: ʻeku (my), hoʻo (your), ʻene (his/her)
Ho-class for things that define you: hoku (my), ho (your), hono (his/her)
With nouns use ʻa (ʻe-class) or ʻo (ho-class) to show possession
Ko + possessive pronoun + verb describes what someone is doing right now

title: Definiteness and the Definitive Accent
preview: Three-level definiteness · accent on the final vowel

teaching.summary: Tongan has three levels of definiteness: indefinite (ha), semi-definite (e, no stress shift), and fully definite (e with stress on the final vowel). The stress shift to the last vowel of a noun phrase is called the definitive accent.

key_rules.rule: Ha marks an indefinite noun; e marks a specific one; e + final stress marks the one both speaker and listener know
key_rules.example_english: Bring the basket. (the one you know about)

key_rules.rule: The definitive accent always falls on the last vowel of the entire noun phrase
Tongan uses the definite article for general categories, unlike English

title: Transitive Word Order
topics: Agent ʻe and object ʻa markers

teaching.summary: When a verb has a definite object, the subject marker changes from ʻa to ʻe. The marker ʻe introduces the doer (agent) and ʻa marks the thing acted upon. Dropping the agent phrase creates a passive meaning.

key_rules.rule: Intransitive subjects take ʻa; transitive subjects take ʻe
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe lau ʻe Sione e tohī.

key_rules.rule: Indefinite objects fuse with the verb and the whole phrase is intransitive
Drop the ʻe phrase to form a passive: the verb stays the same

paradigm.caption: Intransitive vs. Transitive
rows: Naʻe lau ʻe Mele e tohī. (Mele read the book.)
Naʻe lau e tohī. (The book was read.)

title: Numbers and Time
preview: Counting · toko · foʻi · telling time · prices

teaching.summary: Tongan numbers are placed after the noun with the particle ʻe between them. People are counted with the prefix toko. Time uses the ko e pattern with the numeral carrying the definitive accent.

key_rules.rule: Noun + ʻe + number: the particle ʻe links a number to a noun
Use toko before the number when counting people
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e tamaiki ʻe toko fā.

key_rules.rule: Ask the time with ko e fihā eni; answer with ko e + number + eni

teaching.summary: Auxiliary verbs slot in before the main verb. Fie means want to, lava ʻo means be able to, and saiʻia (with ʻi/he) means to like something.

key_rules.rule: Fie goes directly before the main verb to mean want to
Lava ʻo + verb means able to; negate with ʻikai te + pronoun + lava ʻo
Saiʻia he + definite noun means to like something
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ou saiʻia lahi he lea faka-Tongā.
key_rules.example_english: I like the Tongan language a lot.

paradigm.caption: Auxiliary Verb Patterns
rows: ʻOkū ke fie hiva? (Do you want to sing?)
Tē ke lava ʻo haʻu? (Can you come?)
ʻOkū ke saiʻia he hivā? (Do you like the song?)

title: Aspect Markers and Frequency
preview: kei · ʻosi · teʻeki · toe · toki · leva

teaching.summary: Aspect markers go before the verb (except leva, which goes after) to show what stage an action is at: still happening, already done, not yet started, in progress, repeated, or immediate.

key_rules.rule: Kei before the verb means still; often paired with pē
ʻOsi before the verb means already finished; teʻeki ai is the opposite (not yet)
Toe before the verb means again or more
key_rules.example_english: Do you want to come again?

title: Obligation and Necessity
preview: ʻoku totonu ke · kuo pau ke

teaching.summary: ʻOku totonu ke expresses what is right or proper (should). Kuo pau ke expresses what is necessary or certain (must). Both are followed by ke and then the pronoun and verb.

key_rules.rule: ʻOku totonu ke + pronoun + verb means should or ought to
Kuo pau ke + pronoun + verb means must or it is certain that
key_rules.example_tongan: Kuo pau ke u foki ki fale.

key_rules.rule: Negate with ʻoku ʻikai totonu ke for plain should not
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻikai totonu ke ke nofo heni.

paradigm.caption: Obligation Patterns
rows: ʻOku totonu ke u ako. (I should study.)
Kuo pau ke ke ngāue. (You must work.)
ʻOku ʻikai totonu ke u lea. (I should not speak.)

teaching.summary: Pea joins sequential actions (and then), ʻo joins actions treated as one event, ka and kae both mean but (ka before tense markers, kae before verbs), and pe means or.

key_rules.rule: Pea links two actions in sequence; the second tense marker is usually dropped
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ku lotu peā u mohe.

key_rules.rule: ʻO links actions treated as a single combined event
Use ka before a tense marker or pronoun; kae before a verb or adjective
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe ʻalu ʻa Sione kae nofo ʻa Mele.

teaching.summary: Tongan nouns do not change form for plural. Instead, a plural marker goes before the noun: ngaahi for general use, kau for people, fanga for animals, and ʻū for small groups of things.

key_rules.rule: Ngaahi is the default plural marker and works with any noun
Kau is for people; fanga is for animals
Some verbs have special plural forms: ʻalu becomes ō, haʻu becomes ō mai
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe ō ʻa e kau tamaikī ki kolo.

teaching.summary: Tongan uses ke to introduce purpose clauses ("in order to"), he to give reasons ("because"), and maʻa or moʻo to say who benefits from an action.

key_rules.rule: Use ke to express purpose; pronouns after ke take the same forms as after tense markers
key_rules.example_tongan: Kuo nau ō mai ke lau ʻa e tohī.
key_rules.example_english: They have come to read the book.

key_rules.rule: Use he between two clauses to give a reason ("because")
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ou ʻalu ki ʻapi, he ʻoku ou helaʻia.
key_rules.example_english: I am going home, because I am tired.

key_rules.rule: Use maʻa for ʻe-class benefits (food, money) and moʻo for ho-class benefits (house, land)
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻa nau tānaki ʻa e paʻangā maʻa Tēvita.

teaching.summary: Tongan forms comparatives by placing ange after an adjective and superlatives by placing taha after it. The preposition ʻi introduces the thing compared to.

key_rules.rule: Comparative: adjective + ange + ʻa + first thing + ʻi + second thing
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku mālohi ange ʻa Tēvita ʻi Sēmisi.

key_rules.rule: Superlative: adjective + taha (no ʻi phrase needed)
Use tatau + mo for "the same as" and hangē for "like"
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku tatau ʻa Mele mo Seini.
key_rules.example_english: Mele and Seini are the same.

title: Directional Particles
preview: mai · atu · ange · hake · hifo · holo

teaching.summary: Six directional particles attach to verbs to show which way an action moves. Three are person-based (mai, atu, ange) and three are spatial (hake, hifo, holo).

key_rules.rule: Mai marks movement toward the speaker; atu marks movement away
Ange marks movement toward a third person
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOkū ne ʻave ange e katō ki he faiakō.
key_rules.example_english: She is taking the basket to the teacher.

key_rules.rule: Directional particles go right after the verb, before objects or prepositional phrases
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ku ʻave mai e valā mei he falē.
key_rules.example_english: I brought the clothes from the house.

title: The Possessive System in Full
preview: Preposed, postposed, and prepositional possessives unified

teaching.summary: The two possessive classes follow a doer/receiver principle: ʻe-class marks the possessor as the one who acts, ho-class marks the possessor as the one acted upon. This chapter also introduces indefinite possessives.

key_rules.rule: With verbal nouns, ʻe-class means the possessor does the action; ho-class means the action is done to them
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻEku tokoni = my helping; hoku tokoni = my being helped
key_rules.example_english: I do the helping vs. the helping is done to me

key_rules.rule: ʻE-class for things you act on (food, tools); ho-class for things that define you (house, body)
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻEku hele = my knife; hoku fale = my house
key_rules.example_english: My knife (I use it) vs. my house (it shelters me)

key_rules.rule: Indefinite possessives (haʻaku, haʻo, haku, hao) appear in questions and negatives

teaching.summary: This chapter introduces conjunctions for conditions and timing: kapau (if), lolotonga (while), kaeʻoua ke (until), and neongo (although).

key_rules.rule: Kapau introduces a condition; use ʻo kapau when the condition comes after the result
key_rules.example_tongan: Kapau tē ke foki, tē u kuki.
key_rules.example_english: If you return, I will cook.

key_rules.rule: Lolotonga links two simultaneous events ("while")
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOkū ne hiva lolotonga ʻoku ou ngāue.

key_rules.rule: Kaeʻoua ke means "until"
key_rules.example_tongan: Tē u ngāue heni kaeʻoua ke nau foki mai.
key_rules.example_english: I will work here until they come back.

teaching.summary: The back-reference word ai replaces prepositional phrases already understood from context. Combined with ʻi, ʻoku ʻi ai expresses "there is." The classifier foʻi singles out one individual item.

key_rules.rule: ʻOku ʻi ai + ha + noun expresses existence ("there is")
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻi ai ha tangata ʻi heni?

key_rules.rule: Negate existence with ʻoku ʻikai ke ʻi ai + ha + noun
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha meʻakai ʻi fale.
key_rules.example_english: There is no food in the house.

key_rules.rule: The classifier foʻi before a noun singles out one individual item
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻi ai ha foʻi siaine ʻi he kato?
key_rules.example_english: Is there a banana in the basket?

teaching.summary: The prefix faka- is one of Tongan’s most productive word-building tools. Attached to nouns it means "in the manner of"; attached to adjectives or verbs it creates causatives; attached to time nouns it means "every."

key_rules.rule: Faka- + noun creates "in the manner of" or "pertaining to" that noun
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOkū ke poto he hiva faka-Tonga?
key_rules.example_english: Are you good at Tongan singing?

key_rules.rule: Faka- + adjective or verb creates a causative: "to make [adjective]"
key_rules.example_english: She put her child to sleep.

key_rules.rule: Faka- + time noun creates a temporal adverb meaning "every [time period]"

teaching.summary: The word ʻaki expresses the instrument used for an action. The suffix -ʻi turns intransitive verbs into transitive ones that take direct objects, and on already-transitive verbs it emphasizes completion.

key_rules.rule: ʻAki after a verb introduces the instrument
key_rules.example_tongan: Fufulu ʻaki ho falā e vai māfanā.
key_rules.example_english: Wash your mat with warm water.

key_rules.rule: The suffix -ʻi makes an intransitive verb transitive, replacing ki with a direct object
The suffix -ʻaki creates verbs meaning "to use as"
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku mau ngāueʻaki ʻene ʻū falā.

teaching.summary: Tongan reports speech by stringing two clauses together with no connecting word. The verb pehē is the most common introducer. The reportative tokua marks information as secondhand.

key_rules.rule: Verbs of perception introduce reported content by placing two clauses side by side
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ou fanongo ko e kolō lelei ʻa Nukuʻalofa.
key_rules.example_english: I hear that Nukuʻalofa is a good town.

key_rules.rule: Pehē introduces what someone said; the speaker is marked with ʻe
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe pehē ʻe Tēvita ʻe foki ʻa Mele.

key_rules.rule: Tokua at the start signals the information is reported, not personally witnessed
key_rules.example_tongan: Tokua kuo foki ʻa Sione ki Fisi.
key_rules.example_english: It is said that Sione has returned to Fiji.

title: Adjectives and Compound Adjectives
preview: Postposed and preposed · double adjectives · colors

teaching.summary: Attributive adjectives normally follow the noun (ha meʻa lahi). A few fixed words (fuʻu, kiʻi, ʻuluaki, toe) always precede the noun. Color words follow the standard postposed pattern.

key_rules.rule: Standard adjectives go after the noun: article + noun + adjective
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ku fakatau mai ha vaka foʻou.

key_rules.rule: A few adjectives always precede the noun: fuʻu (big), kiʻi (small), ʻuluaki (first), toe (other)
Color words are postposed adjectives
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ku fakatau mai ha sote kulokula.

title: Cleft Sentences and Emphatic Word Order
topics: Ko e meʻa ia ... ai (that's why)

teaching.summary: Moving the subject to the front with ko creates a cleft structure that emphasises who performed the action, similar to English "It was X who..."

key_rules.rule: Cleft with transitive verb: ko + subject + TM + pronoun + verb + object
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko Sēmisi naʻā ne kai e mā.
key_rules.example_english: It was Semisi who ate the bread.

key_rules.rule: Cleft with intransitive verb: ko + subject + TM + verb (no pronoun needed)
key_rules.example_english: It was the children who sang.

key_rules.rule: Ko e meʻa ia ... ai expresses "that’s why" or "that’s the reason"
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e meʻa ia naʻā ku haʻu ai.

paradigm.caption: Standard vs. Cleft Word Order
rows: Naʻe kai ʻe Ana e falā.
Ko Ana naʻā ne kai e falā.

title: Postposed Possessives
preview: ʻaʻaku · ʻoʻoku · ʻa hai? · ʻo hai?

teaching.summary: Possessive pronouns can follow the noun instead of preceding it, adding emphasis to ownership: "That basket is MINE." The ʻe-class uses ʻaʻa- forms and the ho-class uses ʻoʻo- forms.

key_rules.rule: Postposed ʻe-class: ʻaʻaku (mine), ʻaʻau (yours), ʻaʻana (his/hers) follow the noun
Postposed ho-class: ʻoʻoku (mine), ʻoʻou (yours), ʻoʻona (his/hers) for sheltering things
Ask "whose?" with ʻa hai (ʻe-class) or ʻo hai (ho-class)
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e vaka eni ʻa hai?

title: Warnings, Hopes, Permissions, and Uncertainty
preview: tuku ke · ʻofa ke · naʻa (lest / perhaps)

teaching.summary: Tongan has distinct expressions for granting permission (tuku ke), expressing hopes (ʻofa ke), and issuing warnings (naʻa, telia naʻa). The word naʻa doubles as both a warning and a marker of uncertainty.

key_rules.rule: Tuku ke + verb means "let" in the sense of allowing or permitting
ʻOfa pē ke expresses a hope or wish ("may" or "I hope that")
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOfa pē ke mou fiefia ʻi homau kiʻi motū.
key_rules.example_english: May you all be happy on our little island.

key_rules.rule: Naʻa after a command warns of a bad outcome ("lest")
key_rules.example_english: Run away lest you get hurt.

title: Relative Clauses with Ai and Demonstratives
preview: ai in relatives · full 4×4 demonstrative system

teaching.summary: The word ai links a descriptive clause back to a noun, functioning like English "where" or "in which." Tongan also has a full demonstrative system organised by proximity.

key_rules.rule: Ai in a relative clause refers back to a noun; ki ai and mei ai keep the preposition visible
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e kolō ia naʻā ku nofo ai.
key_rules.example_english: That is the village I lived in.

key_rules.rule: Ni/eni = near speaker, na/ena = near listener, ē = pointed at, ia = already mentioned
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻI he falē ni ʻoku mau lotu ai.

key_rules.rule: Naʻa mo means "even"; kaeʻumaʻa means "and also"
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻa mo Mele, naʻe ʻikai fie haʻu mai.
key_rules.example_english: Even Mele did not want to come.

title: Spatial Nouns
preview: ʻi he + loto · lalo · tuʻa · mui · funga · veʻe

teaching.summary: Where English uses prepositions like "inside" or "under," Tongan places spatial nouns directly before the thing being located. The pattern is ʻi he + spatial noun + noun.

key_rules.rule: Spatial noun + noun forms a compound: loto fale (inside the house), lalo tēpile (under the table)
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe tuku e helē ʻi loto falē.
key_rules.example_english: The knife was put inside the house.

key_rules.rule: Funga means "on top of" and veʻe means "right beside"
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku hilī e tohī he funga tēpilē.
key_rules.example_english: The book is on top of the table.

key_rules.rule: With people, insert ʻo before a common noun or ʻia before a proper name
key_rules.example_tongan: Nofo hifo he tafaʻaki ʻo Tēvita!
key_rules.example_english: Sit down by the side of David!

teaching.summary: The same Tongan word can serve as a noun, verb, adjective, or adverb depending on its position in the sentence. No suffixes or conjugation are needed — sentence structure alone determines the role.

key_rules.rule: A noun becomes a verb when placed after a tense marker
A verb placed after a noun acts as an adjective describing that noun
Fai + verbal noun means "to carry out" or "to perform" the action
Ngali means "seems" (firmer); ngalingali means "looks as if" (more tentative)
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ngalingali ʻe ʻuha he ʻahō ni.
key_rules.example_english: It looks like it will rain today.

teaching.summary: This chapter covers practical conversational patterns: asking names and ages, talking about months, expressing "since" with talu and "after" with hilī, and forming ordinal numbers.

key_rules.rule: Ask someone’s name: Ko hai ho hingoā? Answer: Ko [name] hoku hingoā.
Months use ʻi alone with no article: ʻi Sānuali, ʻi Tisema
Talu (mei) means "since" or "ever since"
key_rules.example_tongan: Talu hoʻo haʻū mo ʻene momoko.
key_rules.example_english: Ever since you came, it has been cold.

key_rules.rule: Ordinals use ʻuluaki (first) and ko hono + number (second, third...)

title: The Taʻe- Prefix and Advanced Negation
preview: taʻe- (un- / without) · double negative = emphatic positive

teaching.summary: The prefix taʻe- attaches directly to a word to create its negative, like English "un-" or "without." Several idiomatic expressions (talaʻehai, koloto, neʻineʻi) add nuance beyond simple ʻikai.

key_rules.rule: Taʻe- before a noun means "without": taʻe su (without shoes), taʻe totongi (without pay)
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ngāue taʻe totongi ʻa Sione.

key_rules.rule: Taʻe- before a verb or adjective creates the opposite meaning
ʻIkai + taʻe- together form an emphatic double negative meaning "certainly will"
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻE ʻikai tē u taʻe fai e ngāuē ni.
key_rules.example_english: I will certainly do this work.

key_rules.rule: Neʻineʻi replaces the tense marker and means "no wonder"
key_rules.example_tongan: Neʻineʻi tangi ʻa Seini he naʻe pukē.
key_rules.example_english: No wonder Seini cried — she was sick.

title: The Definitive Accent System
preview: Groups within groups · phrasal and clausal accent

teaching.summary: The definitive accent system marks definiteness by shifting stress to the last vowel of a noun group. As qualifiers and relative clauses extend the group, the accent travels with them.

key_rules.rule: The accent always falls on the very last vowel of the entire noun group
Adverbs modify the verb, not the noun — so the accent does not move onto them
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ke ʻalu ki he fakatahā ʻaneafi?
key_rules.example_english: Did you go to the meeting yesterday?

key_rules.rule: A noun group inside another group gets its own accent at its own end
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e hā e ʻuhinga naʻe ʻita ai ʻa e faiakō kiate aú?
key_rules.example_english: Why was the teacher angry with me?

teaching.summary: When a verb becomes a verbal noun, its preposed pronoun subject changes to a possessive pronoun (ʻe-class). The construction ʻi he + possessive + verbal noun creates temporal clauses meaning "when" or "because."

key_rules.rule: Preposed pronoun becomes ʻe-class possessive: ne → ʻene, ku → ʻeku
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻā ku ofo ʻi heʻene lau ʻa e tohī.
key_rules.example_english: I was surprised when he read the book.

key_rules.rule: Postposed noun subjects stay unchanged in verbal noun constructions
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻI he ʻalu ʻa Sēmisi ki Fisi

key_rules.rule: Ko e + verbal noun + pē means "as soon as"
key_rules.example_tongan: Ko e lava pē ʻeku ngāué, te mau kai.
key_rules.example_english: When my work is done, we will eat.

paradigm.caption: Pronoun → Possessive in Verbal Nouns
rows: ʻi he hoʻo ngāue (when you worked)
ʻi he ʻenau kai (when they ate)

teaching.summary: Tongan divides nouns into personal (people’s names), local (place names), and common (everything else). The class determines which preposition form to use: ʻia/kia/meia before people, standard ʻi/ki/mei before places, and ʻi he/ki he/mei he before common nouns.

key_rules.rule: Before personal nouns: ʻi → ʻia, ki → kia, mei → meia
Before local nouns (places): standard forms ʻi, ki, mei
Some common words act as local nouns when they refer to familiar places
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻi fale = at the house; ʻi he falē = in the (specific) house

title: Conditionals and Counterfactuals
topics: Ka ne ʻikai (if it were not for)

teaching.summary: Ka marks a condition the speaker expects to happen ("when/whenever"). Ka ne marks counterfactual situations ("had you done this"). Kapau leaves the outcome open ("if").

key_rules.rule: Ka + verb expects the condition to happen; translates as "when" or "whenever"
key_rules.example_tongan: Ka haʻu ʻa Siale, pea ke tala ki ai.

key_rules.rule: Ka ne marks counterfactual past: something that did not happen
key_rules.example_tongan: Ka ne u feinga, naʻā ku lava.
key_rules.example_english: Had I tried, I would have succeeded.

key_rules.rule: Ka ne ʻikai means "if it were not for"
key_rules.example_tongan: Ka ne ʻikai ʻa Mele, ʻoku ʻikai lava e ngāuē.
key_rules.example_english: If it were not for Mele, the work could not be done.

paradigm.caption: ka vs. kapau vs. ka ne
rows: Ka haʻu ʻa Siale... (When Siale comes...)
Ka ne u feinga... (Had I tried...)

teaching.summary: Tongan suffixes create new words from existing ones. The suffix -ʻanga names the place of an action, -nga names the thing, and -ʻia means "possessing" or "regarding as."

key_rules.rule: -ʻanga turns a verb into a noun meaning the place or source of the action
-nga turns a verb into a noun meaning the thing itself
key_rules.example_english: from mohe (sleep) and fou (go by a route)

key_rules.rule: -ʻia attached to a noun means possessing or abounding in

teaching.summary: Four prefixes create new verbs: fe-...-ʻaki for reciprocal actions, ma- for potential ability or resultative states, moʻu- for overdoing or overstaying, and kaungā- for doing something together.

key_rules.rule: Fe- + verb + -ʻaki creates reciprocal verbs: doing something to each other
Ma- + verb describes the result of an action, often accidental or unknown agent
key_rules.example_tongan: Naʻe mahae ʻa e kofu ʻo Sialē.

key_rules.rule: Moʻu- + verb + -a means the action continued too long

teaching.summary: Repeating part or all of a word creates new meanings: softened intensity (katakata = smile, from kata = laugh), plurality (kehekehe = various), or intensification (ʻuliʻuli = black, from ʻuli = dirty).

key_rules.rule: Complete reduplication often softens or moderates: katakata (smile) from kata (laugh)
key_rules.example_english: The water is lukewarm. (from māfana = warm)

key_rules.rule: Reduplication can intensify: ʻuliʻuli (black) from ʻuli (dirty)
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻuliʻuli ʻa e langi hē.
key_rules.example_english: The sky over there is black.

key_rules.rule: Faka- + reduplication creates moderated or abstract meanings
key_rules.example_english: The teacher is wise. (from poto = clever)

teaching.summary: The pronoun te/kita serves as an impersonal "one" (anyone in general). Tongan speakers also use dual pronouns for plural audiences to create intimacy, and count everyone in a situation when choosing pronoun number.

key_rules.rule: Te (preposed) and kita (postposed) mean "one" or "oneself" in general statements
key_rules.example_tongan: ʻOku ʻikai totonu ke te tokanga pē kiate kita.
key_rules.example_english: One should not attend only to oneself.

key_rules.rule: Kau is the first-person singular form used at the start of a sentence with no tense marker
Dual pronouns used with plural groups create a personal, intimate tone
key_rules.example_tongan: Ke ta feinga ke fai ʻa e ngāuē.
key_rules.example_english: That we strive to do the work. (said to a group, using dual)

title: Emotional Articles and Possessives
topics: Exclamatory ko e ... ka ko ha ...

teaching.summary: Tongan has emotional articles that do the same job as ordinary articles but carry feelings of pity, affection, or humility. Siʻi replaces the definite article e; siʻa replaces the indefinite article ha.

key_rules.rule: Siʻi replaces e/he to add pity, affection, or sympathy
Siʻa replaces ha for an indefinite noun with the same emotional weight
key_rules.example_english: Has a child been hurt? (poor thing)

key_rules.rule: Emotional possessives prefix siʻ- to regular forms: siʻeku (my, emotional)
key_rules.example_english: My poor little baby is crying.

title: The Language of Respect
preview: Respectful vocabulary · formal register for chiefs

teaching.summary: Tongan has a respect language system called lea fakaʻapaʻapa. Speakers must choose different words depending on the social rank of the person addressed. The system includes the tapu mo acknowledgement formula, five vocabulary levels tied to rank, formal address conventions, and the use of intermediaries when a speaker is uncertain of the correct register.

key_rules.rule: Tapu mo + person/group opens a formal speech or apologises for a sensitive topic
The same concept has different words for different ranks: ordinary, polite, honorific, regal, derogatory
When uncertain of the correct register, use an intermediary rather than guessing
key_rules.example_tongan: (speaker remains silent; intermediary speaks on their behalf)
key_rules.example_english: Using the wrong register causes more offence than remaining silent.
